meta.description: Practical drying and monitoring procedures based on field experience. Cleaned and tagged for consistency with core knowledge base.

knowledgeEntries.title: Daily Monitoring & Adjustment Walk
knowledgeEntries.text: Walk every drying job the exact same way every day. Your goal is to evaluate performance and make immediate corrections.
steps: Start at the dehumidifier and record inlet vs outlet RH and grains
Check that every air mover is running and properly aimed
Take moisture readings in the exact same locations as Day 1
Check for cold spots and musty odors
Make at least one intentional adjustment based on your observations

knowledgeEntries.title: Temperature and RH – Equal Priority
knowledgeEntries.text: Temperature and humidity are equally important. Focus on achieving low grains. Keep the space between 75–85°F while ensuring your dehumidifiers are actively removing moisture.

knowledgeEntries.title: When To Pull Equipment
knowledgeEntries.text: Pull equipment based on meter readings and conditions, not based on how many days the job has been running.

knowledgeEntries.title: Commonly Missed Wet Areas
knowledgeEntries.text: Pay special attention to these frequently missed or slow-to-dry areas: under and behind cabinets, subfloor underneath base plates, California corners, inside vanity kickboards, and under shower pans.

knowledgeEntries.title: Daily Dehumidifier Performance Check
knowledgeEntries.text: Every morning, check inlet vs outlet grains on your dehumidifier. A healthy unit should typically pull 15–25 grains on an active job.

knowledgeEntries.title: Airflow Strategy
knowledgeEntries.text: Generally move air across wet surfaces rather than directly into them. Create consistent circular airflow throughout the affected area.

knowledgeEntries.title: Lower Airflow for Bound Moisture
knowledgeEntries.text: When drying bound moisture in wood, lower velocity airflow is often more effective than high CFM air movers. It allows better heat transfer and creates a more gradual moisture gradient.

knowledgeEntries.title: Match Your Drying Approach to the Loss Type
knowledgeEntries.text: Fast, clean leaks can usually be dried more aggressively. Slow leaks that have had time to penetrate deep into materials should use a more gradual drying approach.

knowledgeEntries.title: Prevent Overheating in Small Spaces
knowledgeEntries.text: Small rooms like bathrooms can easily overheat. Always use a humidistat in small spaces and monitor temperature daily. Never leave a small contained space without temperature control.
